title: Membranes and membrane transport
summary: The plasma membrane is where cell biology meets physical chemistry. This lesson builds B2.1 from the ground up: why phospholipids spontaneously assemble into a bilayer, the fluid mosaic model and the roles of integral proteins, peripheral proteins, cholesterol and glycoproteins, what controls membrane fluidity, and how selective permeability arises. It then works through every transport route — simple diffusion, facilitated diffusion through channel and carrier proteins, osmosis and water potential, active transport and the sodium-potassium pump, and bulk transport by endocytosis and exocytosis. The worked examples and the model answer are laid out exactly the way our marking engine awards Paper 2 marks — one mark per distinct valid point, credit for a correct example, and error-carried-forward — so you can see how each idea earns its mark.
learningObjectives: Explain why phospholipids form a bilayer in water in terms of their hydrophilic heads and hydrophobic tails.
Describe the fluid mosaic model, including integral and peripheral proteins, cholesterol and glycoproteins, and account for membrane fluidity and selective permeability.

Distinguish passive transport (simple diffusion, facilitated diffusion, osmosis) from active transport, and predict cell behaviour in hypertonic, hypotonic and isotonic solutions using water potential.
Outline active transport and the sodium-potassium pump, and describe bulk transport by endocytosis and exocytosis.
Present an explanation so that each distinct valid point earns a mark, with a correct named example and error-carried-forward protecting the score.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: The Cell's Gatekeeper
simpleExplanation.summary: The plasma membrane is a thin, flexible barrier that decides what enters and leaves a cell. It has to be sealed enough to hold the cell's contents together, yet selective enough to let the right substances cross at the right rate — and that balance comes straight from the way its molecules are arranged.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Picture the security wall around a busy venue. The solid wall itself is the phospholipid bilayer: it keeps the inside separate from the outside. A few tiny, easily-dissolved visitors slip straight through the fabric of the wall (simple diffusion). Others can only enter through a specific gate, and each gate fits only one kind of visitor (facilitated diffusion). Some visitors have to be pushed in against a crowd streaming the other way, which takes a paid guard doing work (active transport, powered by ATP). And a whole coach-load arriving at once is admitted through a special loading bay that opens, swallows the coach and reseals (endocytosis).
steps: Phospholipids have a water-loving head and two water-fearing tails, so in water they line up into a bilayer with the tails tucked away from water — this happens spontaneously and gives the membrane its basic sealed sheet.

The fluid mosaic model adds proteins, cholesterol and glycoproteins to that sheet; components drift sideways (fluid) and vary across the membrane (mosaic), which is why the membrane can carry out so many jobs.
Passive transport moves substances down their concentration or water-potential gradient with no ATP: simple diffusion, facilitated diffusion and osmosis.
Active transport uses ATP and protein pumps to move substances against the gradient, while bulk transport packages large materials into vesicles for endocytosis (in) and exocytosis (out).

flashcards.back: An amphipathic molecule with a hydrophilic (polar) phosphate head and two hydrophobic (nonpolar) fatty-acid tails. This split personality is what drives bilayer formation in water.

In water the hydrophilic heads face outward toward the aqueous surroundings while the hydrophobic tails cluster inward away from water. The bilayer is the arrangement that is most stable in water — it forms spontaneously, no energy input needed.
The membrane is a FLUID bilayer of phospholipids in which proteins and other molecules drift laterally, forming a MOSAIC of many different components (Singer and Nicolson, 1972).

Integral proteins are embedded in the hydrophobic core, often spanning the whole membrane (transmembrane); many are transporters. Peripheral proteins sit on a surface, attached to the heads or to an integral protein, and are not embedded in the core.

A lipid wedged between phospholipids in animal-cell membranes that buffers fluidity: it stops the membrane becoming too fluid when warm and too rigid when cold, keeping it stable across a range of temperatures.
A membrane protein with a carbohydrate chain attached, projecting from the outer surface. Glycoproteins act as cell-recognition markers and receptors — molecular ID tags read by other cells.

The membrane lets some substances cross freely while restricting others. Small nonpolar molecules cross the bilayer easily; ions and large polar molecules need specific proteins — so the cell controls its internal composition.
Net movement of small nonpolar molecules (e.g. O₂, CO₂) directly through the bilayer from higher to lower concentration. Passive: no ATP, no protein needed.
PASSIVE movement of ions or large polar molecules (e.g. glucose) down their gradient THROUGH a channel or carrier protein. Still no ATP — the protein only provides a route, it does not supply energy.

A channel protein is a hydrophilic pore that specific ions pass straight through. A carrier protein binds the substance and changes shape to move it across. Both are used in facilitated diffusion; carriers are also used in active transport.

The net movement of WATER only across a partially permeable membrane, from a region of higher water potential to a region of lower water potential. It is a passive process — a special case of diffusion, for water.

Water potential falls as solute concentration rises. A HYPERTONIC solution has lower water potential than the cell (cell loses water); a HYPOTONIC solution has higher water potential (cell gains water); an ISOTONIC solution has equal water potential (no net movement).
Movement of a substance AGAINST its concentration gradient, requiring ATP and a specific carrier protein (pump). Because it works against the gradient it is not spontaneous and must be powered.
An active-transport pump using ATP to move 3 Na⁺ out of the cell and 2 K⁺ in, both against their gradients. It maintains the ion gradients used in nerve impulses and in co-transport of glucose.

Bulk transport of large materials in vesicles, needing energy and membrane fluidity. Endocytosis takes material IN by infolding the membrane to form a vesicle; exocytosis expels material as a vesicle fuses with the membrane and releases its contents.

Osmosis is the movement of WATER, not solute. Never write 'the sugar moves in by osmosis' — the water moves toward the higher solute concentration (lower water potential); the solute is what stays put.

flashcards.front: Exam Trap: facilitated diffusion is passive
flashcards.back: Using a protein does NOT make it active. Facilitated diffusion still runs down the gradient and uses no ATP. Only movement AGAINST the gradient with ATP counts as active transport.

quickCheck.prompt: A student writes that 'glucose moves into a cell by osmosis when there is more glucose outside'. What is wrong with this statement?
options: Nothing — this is a correct use of osmosis
Osmosis is the movement of water, not solute; glucose would enter by facilitated diffusion
Osmosis only happens in plant cells
Glucose cannot cross a membrane at all
quickCheck.answer: Osmosis is the movement of water, not solute; glucose would enter by facilitated diffusion

quickCheck.prompt: Facilitated diffusion uses a membrane protein. Does this make it a form of active transport?
options: Yes — any process using a protein is active transport
Yes — the protein supplies energy to move the molecule
No — it moves substances down the gradient and uses no ATP, so it is passive
No — but only because it moves ions rather than glucose
quickCheck.answer: No — it moves substances down the gradient and uses no ATP, so it is passive

quickCheck.prompt: An animal cell is placed in a HYPERTONIC solution. What happens to the cell?
options: It swells and may burst, because water enters
It shrinks (crenates), because water leaves by osmosis
Nothing, because the solutions are in balance
It takes in solute by active transport until balanced
quickCheck.answer: It shrinks (crenates), because water leaves by osmosis

quickCheck.prompt: Which statement about the sodium-potassium pump is correct?
options: It moves ions down their gradients, so it needs no energy
It moves ions against their gradients and requires ATP
It moves water against its gradient using ATP
It is an example of simple diffusion through the bilayer
quickCheck.answer: It moves ions against their gradients and requires ATP

quickCheck.prompt: Why can oxygen cross the phospholipid bilayer directly, while sodium ions cannot?
options: Oxygen is charged and sodium is not
Oxygen is small and nonpolar, so it passes through the hydrophobic core; the charged Na⁺ is repelled and needs a protein
Sodium is smaller than oxygen so it bounces off
Both cross equally easily; the question is based on a false premise
quickCheck.answer: Oxygen is small and nonpolar, so it passes through the hydrophobic core; the charged Na⁺ is repelled and needs a protein

faq.q: Why do phospholipids form a bilayer instead of just floating around?
faq.a: Each phospholipid is amphipathic: its phosphate head is hydrophilic and its two fatty-acid tails are hydrophobic. In water the tails cannot mix with the surrounding molecules, so the system is most stable when the tails hide away from water and the heads face it. A double layer — heads out on both faces, tails sandwiched in the middle — achieves exactly that, which is why the bilayer assembles spontaneously with no energy input. The same physics is why a torn membrane reseals itself.

faq.q: What is the difference between a channel protein and a carrier protein?
faq.a: Both are used in facilitated diffusion. A channel protein is a hydrophilic pore that lets specific ions or small molecules pass straight through when it is open. A carrier protein binds its target on one side of the membrane, changes shape, and releases it on the other side. The key extra point is that carrier proteins are also used in active transport — when driven by ATP, the same shape-change can pump a substance against its gradient.

faq.q: How is osmosis different from diffusion?
faq.a: Osmosis is really a special case of diffusion that applies only to water across a partially permeable membrane. In diffusion we track any substance moving down its concentration gradient; in osmosis we track water moving from higher to lower water potential. The common exam error is to describe solute 'moving by osmosis' — only the water moves in osmosis, toward the region of higher solute concentration.

faq.q: Do plant cells burst in a hypotonic solution like animal cells do?
faq.a: No, and the reason is the cell wall. In a hypotonic solution water enters both plant and animal cells by osmosis. An animal cell keeps swelling and can lyse (burst). A plant cell swells until it presses against its rigid cellulose cell wall; the wall pushes back, generating turgor pressure that stops further water entry, so the cell becomes turgid rather than bursting. This turgor is what keeps non-woody plants upright — lose it and the plant wilts.

sections.content: Every cell has to be both sealed and open at once: sealed enough to keep its carefully controlled contents from mixing with the surroundings, yet open enough to take in nutrients, expel waste and respond to signals. The plasma membrane resolves that tension. It is only a few nanometres thick, yet its molecular design lets it act as a selective barrier that decides which substances cross and how fast. This lesson starts with why the membrane has the structure it does, then works through each transport route in turn. As in the exemplar topics, the worked examples and the model answer are set out the way marks are actually awarded — because in an explain-heavy topic, HOW you phrase each point decides how many marks you keep.
The phospholipid bilayer and why it forms

The foundation of every membrane is the phospholipid. Each phospholipid molecule is amphipathic — it has two chemically opposite ends. One end is a phosphate-containing head that is hydrophilic (polar, water-attracting); the other end is a pair of fatty-acid tails that are hydrophobic (nonpolar, water-repelling). Place many phospholipids in a watery environment and they arrange themselves so that the hydrophilic heads face the water while the hydrophobic tails huddle together away from it. The lowest-energy, most stable arrangement is a bilayer: two sheets of phospholipids tail-to-tail, with heads facing the aqueous fluids inside and outside the cell and the tails forming a nonpolar core in the middle. This assembly is spontaneous — it needs no energy — and it is self-sealing, because any gap exposes tails to water and is immediately closed.

items: **Amphipathic phospholipids:** hydrophilic phosphate head, hydrophobic fatty-acid tails.
**Heads out, tails in:** heads face the aqueous fluids either side; tails cluster into a nonpolar core, hidden from water.
**Spontaneous and self-sealing:** the bilayer is the most stable arrangement in water, so it forms without energy and reseals when disrupted.
**Consequence — selective barrier:** the hydrophobic core blocks charged and large polar molecules, which is where selective permeability begins.

sections.content: The fluid mosaic model

The accepted description of membrane structure is the fluid mosaic model, proposed by Singer and Nicolson in 1972. 'Fluid' captures the fact that the phospholipids, and many of the proteins, are not fixed — they drift sideways within the plane of the membrane, so the whole sheet behaves like a two-dimensional liquid. 'Mosaic' captures the fact that the membrane is a patchwork of many different components set into the bilayer. Alongside the phospholipids sit integral proteins, embedded in the hydrophobic core and often spanning the whole membrane as transmembrane transporters; peripheral proteins, attached loosely to a surface or to an integral protein without entering the core; cholesterol, a lipid wedged between phospholipids in animal cells; and glycoproteins, proteins bearing carbohydrate chains that project from the outer surface as cell-recognition markers.

items: **Phospholipid bilayer:** the fluid fabric of the membrane.
**Integral proteins:** embedded in the core, often transmembrane; carry out transport and act as receptors and enzymes.
**Peripheral proteins:** attached to a surface or to an integral protein, not embedded in the core; often structural or enzymatic.
**Cholesterol:** wedged between phospholipids (animal cells); buffers fluidity.
**Glycoproteins:** carbohydrate-tagged proteins on the outer face acting as recognition markers and receptors.

sections.content: Membrane fluidity

Fluidity is not an incidental detail — it is essential for transport, for vesicle formation and for membranes to fuse and reseal. Two main factors control it. The first is the fatty-acid tails: tails with double bonds (unsaturated) have kinks that stop the phospholipids packing tightly, keeping the membrane more fluid, while straight saturated tails pack closely and make it more rigid. The second is cholesterol, which acts as a fluidity buffer in animal cells. At higher temperatures cholesterol restrains the phospholipids and stops the membrane becoming too fluid; at lower temperatures it keeps them apart and stops the membrane solidifying. The result is a membrane that stays workable across the range of temperatures a cell experiences.
Selective permeability

Because the middle of the bilayer is hydrophobic, the membrane is selectively permeable: it lets some substances cross freely and restricts others. Small nonpolar molecules such as oxygen and carbon dioxide dissolve into the hydrophobic core and pass straight through. Small uncharged polar molecules like water cross slowly. But ions and large polar molecules such as glucose are repelled by the core and can only cross with the help of transport proteins. This selectivity is the whole point of the membrane: by controlling which substances cross, and by using proteins that can be regulated, the cell maintains an internal composition quite different from its surroundings.

Transport across the membrane falls into two broad categories. Passive transport moves substances DOWN a gradient and needs no metabolic energy; it includes simple diffusion, facilitated diffusion and osmosis. Active transport moves substances AGAINST a gradient and therefore requires energy from ATP together with a protein pump. Bulk transport handles materials too large for either route by packaging them into vesicles.
Passive transport: simple diffusion, facilitated diffusion and osmosis

Passive transport is driven by the random thermal motion of particles. Because particles spread out from where they are crowded to where they are sparse, the net movement is always DOWN the concentration gradient, from higher to lower concentration, until the two sides are equal. No ATP is spent — the energy comes free from the particles' own motion. There are three passive routes across the plasma membrane.

items: **Simple diffusion:** small nonpolar molecules (O₂, CO₂) pass directly through the bilayer down their gradient. No protein, no ATP.
**Facilitated diffusion:** ions and large polar molecules (glucose) move down their gradient THROUGH a channel or carrier protein. The protein provides a route but no energy — it is still passive.
**Osmosis:** the net movement of WATER only, across a partially permeable membrane, from higher water potential to lower water potential. Passive, and a special case of diffusion for water.

sections.content: Osmosis is best described using water potential. Water potential is a measure of the tendency of water to move; pure water has the highest value, and adding solute lowers it. Water always moves by osmosis from higher water potential to lower water potential — which means, in everyday terms, toward the more concentrated solution. Comparing a cell with its surroundings gives three cases, described by tonicity. In a HYPERTONIC solution the outside has lower water potential than the cell, so water leaves the cell. In a HYPOTONIC solution the outside has higher water potential, so water enters the cell. In an ISOTONIC solution the water potentials are equal, so there is no net movement.

items: **Hypertonic surroundings → water leaves:** an animal cell shrinks/crenates; a plant cell loses turgor and becomes flaccid, then plasmolysed if severe.
**Hypotonic surroundings → water enters:** an animal cell swells and may lyse (burst); a plant cell becomes turgid, held in check by its cell wall.
**Isotonic surroundings → no net movement:** cell volume stays constant.

sections.question: A red blood cell (which has no cell wall) is placed in a concentrated salt solution that is hypertonic to its cytoplasm. Predict and explain what happens to the cell. Then state what would happen to a plant cell placed in the same solution. [4]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
The salt solution is hypertonic, so it has a LOWER water potential than the cytoplasm of the red blood cell. Water therefore moves out of the cell by osmosis, from the higher water potential inside to the lower water potential outside, across the partially permeable membrane. As the cell loses water its cytoplasm shrinks and the cell crenates (shrivels); because a red blood cell has no cell wall, there is nothing to resist this, so the cell simply shrinks. A plant cell placed in the same solution would also lose water by osmosis, but its rigid cellulose cell wall stops it collapsing entirely — instead the cell loses turgor and becomes flaccid, and in a strongly hypertonic solution the cell membrane pulls away from the wall (plasmolysis).

---
**How our marking engine awards the 4 marks** — each distinct valid point scores 1 (maximum 4):

- **Point 1 (M):** the solution is hypertonic / has lower water potential than the cell.
- **Point 2 (M):** water leaves the cell by OSMOSIS, from higher to lower water potential (state that it is water moving, not solute).
- **Point 3 (A):** correct prediction for the red blood cell — it shrinks / crenates (accept 'shrivels').
- **Point 4 (A):** correct contrast for the plant cell — it becomes flaccid / plasmolysed because the cell wall prevents it shrinking like the animal cell.

*The most common way to lose Point 2 is to write that 'salt moves into the cell' — the solute does not move in osmosis; the WATER moves out. State the direction in terms of water potential and you protect the mark.*

sections.content: In osmosis questions, always name the substance that moves (WATER) and the direction (from higher to lower water potential). Vague phrases like 'the solutions balance out' or 'the salt moves across' throw away marks. Compare the water potentials first, then predict the water movement, then state the effect on the cell — that ordering earns each mark in turn.
Active transport and the sodium-potassium pump

Sometimes a cell needs to move a substance the 'wrong' way — against its concentration gradient, from where it is scarce to where it is already abundant. This does not happen spontaneously, so it must be powered: active transport uses energy from ATP together with a specific carrier protein, called a pump. The ATP causes the pump to change shape, carrying the bound substance across and releasing it on the far side, then resetting. Because it depends on ATP, active transport stops if respiration is inhibited — a useful experimental way to tell it apart from passive transport, which continues regardless.

The classic example is the sodium-potassium pump, found in the membranes of animal cells. Using the energy from one ATP, it pumps 3 sodium ions (Na⁺) out of the cell and 2 potassium ions (K⁺) into the cell, both against their concentration gradients. This maintains a low internal Na⁺ and high internal K⁺, setting up the electrochemical gradients that nerve cells use to transmit impulses and that other cells use to drive the co-transport of substances such as glucose.

items: **Against the gradient:** active transport moves substances from lower to higher concentration.
**Requires ATP:** the energy from ATP drives the shape change of the carrier protein (pump).
**Uses a specific carrier protein:** each pump transports particular substances.
**Sodium-potassium pump:** 3 Na⁺ out and 2 K⁺ in per ATP, maintaining ion gradients for nerve impulses and co-transport.

sections.content: Bulk transport: endocytosis and exocytosis

Some materials are simply too large to cross the membrane through the bilayer or a protein — whole proteins, polysaccharides, or even entire microorganisms. These are moved by bulk transport, which relies on the membrane's fluidity to form and fuse vesicles, and which requires energy. In endocytosis the membrane folds inward around the material, pinches off, and forms a vesicle that carries the material into the cytoplasm; when the material is solid this is called phagocytosis ('cell eating') and when it is dissolved it is pinocytosis ('cell drinking'). Exocytosis is the reverse: a vesicle carrying material — for example, a secreted protein — moves to the plasma membrane, fuses with it, and releases its contents to the outside. Both processes let cells handle cargo far larger than any single molecule that diffusion or pumping could manage.

items: **Endocytosis:** membrane infolds and pinches off a vesicle, bringing large material INTO the cell (phagocytosis of solids, pinocytosis of liquids).
**Exocytosis:** a vesicle fuses with the membrane and releases its contents OUT of the cell (e.g. secretion of proteins).
**Requires energy and membrane fluidity:** vesicles must form, move and fuse — impossible in a rigid membrane.

sections.question: Compare the surface-area-to-volume ratio of a small cubic cell of side 2 µm with a larger cubic cell of side 8 µm, and explain the significance of the difference for exchange across the membrane. [4]
sections.solution:
**Step 1 — small cell (side s = 2 µm).**
Surface area $= 6s^2 = 6 \times 2^2 = 24\ \text{µm}^2$; volume $= s^3 = 2^3 = 8\ \text{µm}^3$.
SA:V $= 24 / 8 = 3.0$.  [M1: correct SA and V for one cell]

**Step 2 — large cell (side s = 8 µm).**
Surface area $= 6 \times 8^2 = 384\ \text{µm}^2$; volume $= 8^3 = 512\ \text{µm}^3$.
SA:V $= 384 / 512 = 0.75$.  [A1: both ratios correct — 3.0 and 0.75]

**Step 3 — interpret.**
The smaller cell has the much larger surface-area-to-volume ratio. [M1]

**Step 4 — significance.**
A larger SA:V means more membrane is available per unit of cytoplasm, so substances can be exchanged (by diffusion, osmosis and active transport) fast enough to supply the whole volume's needs and remove its wastes. As a cell grows, volume rises faster than surface area, the ratio falls, and exchange across the membrane may become too slow to serve the interior — which limits how large a single cell can be. [A1: links high SA:V to efficient exchange and a limit on cell size]

*If your ratios were wrong but you correctly stated that a higher SA:V means faster exchange relative to volume, the interpretation marks are still awarded by error-carried-forward — the method is what the engine credits.*

sections.content: Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Saying solute 'moves by osmosis'** — osmosis is the movement of WATER only. Write that water moves from higher to lower water potential; the solute stays put.

**Calling facilitated diffusion 'active'** — using a protein does not make it active. Facilitated diffusion runs DOWN the gradient with NO ATP, so it is passive. Only movement against the gradient using ATP is active transport.
**Forgetting to state ATP for active transport** — you must say active transport requires ATP AND moves substances against the concentration gradient. Leaving out either half loses the mark.

**Predicting the wrong cell result for tonicity** — a HYPERTONIC solution makes a cell LOSE water and shrink; a HYPOTONIC solution makes it gain water and swell. Mixing these up reverses the whole answer.

**Ignoring the cell wall in plant-cell osmosis** — a plant cell in a hypotonic solution becomes turgid (not burst) because the wall resists; in a hypertonic solution it plasmolyses. Treating a plant cell like an animal cell loses marks.
**Describing the membrane as a solid 'wall'** — it is a FLUID mosaic; the fluidity is what allows transport proteins to work and vesicles to form and fuse.
**Confusing channel and carrier proteins** — a channel is an open pore for ions; a carrier changes shape to move its cargo. Only carriers are used in active transport.

sections.content: Model answer — marked the way our engine marks it

B2.1 asks you to explain processes, and explanation marks are awarded analytically — each distinct valid biological point is worth one mark. Method-style points (M) credit correct reasoning about a mechanism, answer-style points (A) credit a correct named example or conclusion, and error-carried-forward (ECF) means an early slip does not cost the marks that follow, provided your reasoning is written down. Notice how every mark below is tied to a specific named idea, not to loose phrasing, and how the engine accepts equivalent correct wording.

sections.question: Explain how substances move across the plasma membrane by passive and active transport. [4]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
In passive transport, substances move DOWN their concentration gradient (or, for water, from higher to lower water potential) without any input of ATP: this includes simple diffusion of small nonpolar molecules directly through the bilayer, facilitated diffusion of ions and large polar molecules through channel or carrier proteins, and osmosis, the movement of water across a partially permeable membrane. In active transport, substances move AGAINST their concentration gradient, from lower to higher concentration, which is not spontaneous and therefore requires energy from ATP together with a specific carrier protein (a pump). A named example of active transport is the sodium-potassium pump, which uses ATP to move 3 Na⁺ out of the cell and 2 K⁺ in, both against their gradients.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 4 marks** — each distinct valid point scores 1 (maximum 4):

- **Point 1 (M) — passive definition:** passive transport moves substances DOWN the gradient (or water from high to low water potential) with NO ATP.
- **Point 2 (M) — a passive mechanism named:** any correct one of simple diffusion / facilitated diffusion (through channel or carrier proteins) / osmosis.
- **Point 3 (M) — active definition:** active transport moves substances AGAINST the gradient using ATP and a protein pump. Both halves — 'against the gradient' AND 'ATP' — must be present for this mark.
- **Point 4 (A) — a correct example:** a valid named example of transport, most obviously the sodium-potassium pump for active transport (accept any correct named example, e.g. glucose by facilitated diffusion for the passive side).

Because more than four creditable points are available (two passive mechanisms, the passive definition, the active definition and a named example), the engine caps the award at 4 — there is deliberate room to miss a point and still reach full marks. But the marks are for DISTINCT ideas: naming diffusion twice in different words scores once.

**Equivalents accepted.** The engine accepts equivalent correct wording — 'requires energy from ATP' or 'uses metabolic energy from respiration', 'against the gradient' or 'from low to high concentration', 'partially permeable' or 'semi-permeable'. It credits the biology, not a fixed phrase.

**ECF and partial credit.** The points are independent, so a student who muddles one mechanism but correctly contrasts passive (down, no ATP) with active (against, ATP + pump) and gives a valid example still banks those points. There is no all-or-nothing penalty.

**What the engine rejects.** Saying only 'substances move across the membrane' with no direction, or calling facilitated diffusion 'active' because it uses a protein, scores nothing for that point — the mark scheme needs the direction (down vs against the gradient) and the energy status (no ATP vs ATP).

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR answer this same way — point by point, with ECF, accepting equivalents, and capping at the marks available. So write in distinct, creditable statements: contrast passive (down the gradient, no ATP) with active (against the gradient, ATP + pump), name at least one mechanism, and give a correct example such as the sodium-potassium pump. Each named idea is a mark; a vague sentence that names neither direction nor energy earns nothing.
Where this leads

The transport principles here underpin much of the rest of biology. Facilitated diffusion and active transport reappear in the absorption of nutrients in the gut, in reabsorption in the kidney, and in the loading of ions in plant roots. The sodium-potassium pump is the engine behind the resting potential and nerve impulses. Osmosis and water potential govern water uptake in plants and the fate of cells in medical fluids. Master the two questions — which way is the substance moving relative to its gradient, and does the cell have to spend ATP — and you have a framework that explains membrane transport in every one of those contexts.
